description: Select all employees who earn more than 50000. The query has a syntax error with a dangling AND.
schema_sql: CREATE TABLE employees (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL, salary REAL NOT NULL);
seed_data_sql: INSERT INTO employees VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'Engineering', 75000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (2, 'Bob', 'Marketing', 45000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (3, 'Carol', 'Engineering', 90000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (4, 'Dave', 'HR', 55000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (5, 'Eve', 'Marketing', 48000);
broken_query: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE salary > 50000 AND
expected_query: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE salary > 50000
schema_description:
Table: employees
  - id (INTEGER): Primary key
  - name (TEXT): Employee name
  - department (TEXT): Department name
  - salary (REAL): Annual salary
expected_description: Returns the names of employees whose salary is greater than 50000. Should return 3 rows: Alice, Carol, Dave.

description: Select product name and price from the products table, but the query references a non-existent column 'cost' instead of 'price'.
schema_sql: CREATE TABLE products (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, category TEXT NOT NULL, price REAL NOT NULL, stock INTEGER NOT NULL);
seed_data_sql: INSERT INTO products VALUES (1, 'Laptop', 'Electronics', 999.99, 50); INSERT INTO products VALUES (2, 'Mouse', 'Electronics', 29.99, 200); INSERT INTO products VALUES (3, 'Desk', 'Furniture', 349.99, 30); INSERT INTO products VALUES (4, 'Chair', 'Furniture', 199.99, 45); INSERT INTO products VALUES (5, 'Keyboard', 'Electronics', 79.99, 150);
broken_query: SELECT name, cost FROM products WHERE category = 'Electronics'
expected_query: SELECT name, price FROM products WHERE category = 'Electronics'
schema_description:
Table: products
  - id (INTEGER): Primary key
  - name (TEXT): Product name
  - category (TEXT): Product category
  - price (REAL): Product price
  - stock (INTEGER): Units in stock
expected_description: Returns name and price of all Electronics products. Should return 3 rows: Laptop (999.99), Mouse (29.99), Keyboard (79.99).

description: Get each customer's name and their order total, but the JOIN condition is wrong — it joins on orders.id = customers.id instead of orders.customer_id = customers.id.
schema_sql: CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL); CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL, total REAL NOT NULL, status TEXT NOT NULL);
seed_data_sql: INSERT INTO customers VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'New York'); INSERT INTO customers VALUES (2, 'Bob', 'Chicago'); INSERT INTO customers VALUES (3, 'Carol', 'San Francisco'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (1, 2, 150.00, 'shipped'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (2, 1, 320.50, 'delivered'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (3, 3, 89.99, 'pending'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (4, 1, 450.00, 'delivered');
broken_query: SELECT customers.name, orders.total FROM orders JOIN customers ON orders.id = customers.id
expected_query: SELECT customers.name, orders.total FROM orders JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.id
schema_description:
Table: customers
  - id (INTEGER): Primary key
  - name (TEXT): Customer name
  - city (TEXT): City

Table: orders
  - id (INTEGER): Primary key
  - customer_id (INTEGER): Foreign key referencing customers.id
  - total (REAL): Order total amount
  - status (TEXT): Order status
expected_description: Returns each customer's name alongside their order total. Should return 4 rows pairing each order with the correct customer name.

description: Find all students who passed (score >= 60) in the 'Mathematics' course, but the WHERE clause uses the wrong condition — it filters on score < 60 instead of score >= 60.
schema_sql: CREATE TABLE students (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, grade TEXT NOT NULL); CREATE TABLE enrollments (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, student_id INTEGER NOT NULL, course TEXT NOT NULL, score REAL NOT NULL);
seed_data_sql: INSERT INTO students VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'A'); INSERT INTO students VALUES (2, 'Bob', 'B'); INSERT INTO students VALUES (3, 'Carol', 'A'); INSERT INTO students VALUES (4, 'Dave', 'C'); INSERT INTO enrollments VALUES (1, 1, 'Mathematics', 92.0); INSERT INTO enrollments VALUES (2, 2, 'Mathematics', 55.0); INSERT INTO enrollments VALUES (3, 3, 'Mathematics', 78.0); INSERT INTO enrollments VALUES (4, 4, 'Mathematics', 41.0); INSERT INTO enrollments VALUES (5, 1, 'Physics', 88.0);
broken_query: SELECT students.name FROM students JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id WHERE enrollments.course = 'Mathematics' AND enrollments.score < 60
expected_query: SELECT students.name FROM students JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id WHERE enrollments.course = 'Mathematics' AND enrollments.score >= 60
schema_description:
Table: students
  - id (INTEGER): Primary key
  - name (TEXT): Student name
  - grade (TEXT): Overall grade

Table: enrollments
  - id (INTEGER): Primary key
  - student_id (INTEGER): Foreign key referencing students.id
  - course (TEXT): Course name
  - score (REAL): Score achieved
expected_description: Returns names of students who passed Mathematics (score >= 60). Should return 2 rows: Alice, Carol.

description: Find the highest-paid employee in each department using a window function. The broken query omits PARTITION BY department, so ROW_NUMBER ranks all employees globally instead of within each department, returning only the single top earner company-wide.
schema_sql: CREATE TABLE employees (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL, salary REAL NOT NULL, hire_year INTEGER NOT NULL);
seed_data_sql: INSERT INTO employees VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'Engineering', 95000, 2019); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (2, 'Bob', 'Engineering', 87000, 2020); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (3, 'Charlie', 'Engineering', 102000, 2018); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (4, 'Diana', 'Marketing', 65000, 2021); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (5, 'Eve', 'Marketing', 58000, 2022); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (6, 'Frank', 'Marketing', 61000, 2020); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (7, 'Grace', 'Finance', 81000, 2019); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (8, 'Henry', 'Finance', 78000, 2021); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (9, 'Iris', 'HR', 55000, 2022); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (10, 'Jack', 'HR', 52000, 2023); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (11, 'Kate', 'HR', 57000, 2021); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (12, 'Leo', 'Finance', 85000, 2018);
broken_query: SELECT name, department, salary FROM (SELECT name, department, salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn FROM employees) ranked WHERE rn = 1
expected_query: SELECT name, department, salary FROM (SELECT name, department, salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn FROM employees) ranked WHERE rn = 1 ORDER BY department
schema_description:
Table: employees
  - id (INTEGER): Primary key
  - name (TEXT): Employee name
  - department (TEXT): Department name (Engineering, Marketing, Finance, HR)
  - salary (REAL): Annual salary
  - hire_year (INTEGER): Year hired
expected_description: Returns the highest-paid employee in each department. Should return 4 rows: Charlie/Engineering/102000, Leo/Finance/85000, Kate/HR/57000, Diana/Marketing/65000.

description: Using a CTE, find customers whose total lifetime spending exceeds the average total spending across all customers. The broken query compares against AVG(amount) — the mean of individual order amounts — instead of the mean of per-customer totals, which overstates the threshold and incorrectly includes Dave.
schema_sql: CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, country TEXT NOT NULL); CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL, product TEXT NOT NULL, amount REAL NOT NULL, order_date TEXT NOT NULL);
seed_data_sql: INSERT INTO customers VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'USA'); INSERT INTO customers VALUES (2, 'Bob', 'UK'); INSERT INTO customers VALUES (3, 'Carol', 'Canada'); INSERT INTO customers VALUES (4, 'Dave', 'USA'); INSERT INTO customers VALUES (5, 'Emma', 'UK'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (1, 1, 'Laptop', 180.0, '2024-01-05'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (2, 1, 'Monitor', 240.0, '2024-02-10'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (3, 1, 'Keyboard', 90.0, '2024-03-15'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (4, 2, 'Mouse', 50.0, '2024-01-20'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (5, 2, 'Headset', 35.0, '2024-02-28'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (6, 3, 'Desk', 320.0, '2024-01-08'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (7, 3, 'Chair', 180.0, '2024-02-14'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (8, 3, 'Lamp', 260.0, '2024-03-22'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (9, 3, 'Shelf', 140.0, '2024-04-01'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (10, 4, 'Tablet', 100.0, '2024-01-30'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (11, 4, 'Case', 115.0, '2024-02-18'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (12, 4, 'Stand', 95.0, '2024-03-10'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (13, 5, 'Camera', 420.0, '2024-01-12'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (14, 5, 'Tripod', 60.0, '2024-02-25');
broken_query: WITH customer_totals AS (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id) SELECT c.name, ct.total_spent FROM customers c JOIN customer_totals ct ON c.id = ct.customer_id WHERE ct.total_spent > (SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders) ORDER BY ct.total_spent DESC
expected_query: WITH customer_totals AS (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id) SELECT c.name, ct.total_spent FROM customers c JOIN customer_totals ct ON c.id = ct.customer_id WHERE ct.total_spent > (SELECT AVG(total_spent) FROM customer_totals) ORDER BY ct.total_spent DESC
schema_description:
Table: customers
  - id (INTEGER): Primary key
  - name (TEXT): Customer name
  - country (TEXT): Country

Table: orders
  - id (INTEGER): Primary key
  - customer_id (INTEGER): Foreign key referencing customers.id
  - product (TEXT): Product purchased
  - amount (REAL): Order amount
  - order_date (TEXT): Date of order (YYYY-MM-DD)
expected_description: Returns customers whose total spending exceeds the average per-customer total (457.0). Should return 3 rows ordered by total_spent DESC: Carol/900.0, Alice/510.0, Emma/480.0. Note: Dave (310.0) is below the average total and must be excluded.

description: Compute month-over-month revenue change using LAG. The broken query orders the LAG window by amount value (ascending) instead of chronologically by (year, month), so 'previous month revenue' is actually the next-lower revenue in sorted order, not the actual prior month.
schema_sql: CREATE TABLE monthly_revenue (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, year INTEGER NOT NULL, month INTEGER NOT NULL, amount REAL NOT NULL);
seed_data_sql: INSERT INTO monthly_revenue VALUES (1, 2024, 1, 42000); INSERT INTO monthly_revenue VALUES (2, 2024, 2, 38000); INSERT INTO monthly_revenue VALUES (3, 2024, 3, 45000); INSERT INTO monthly_revenue VALUES (4, 2024, 4, 51000); INSERT INTO monthly_revenue VALUES (5, 2024, 5, 48000); INSERT INTO monthly_revenue VALUES (6, 2024, 6, 55000); INSERT INTO monthly_revenue VALUES (7, 2024, 7, 52000); INSERT INTO monthly_revenue VALUES (8, 2024, 8, 60000); INSERT INTO monthly_revenue VALUES (9, 2024, 9, 57000); INSERT INTO monthly_revenue VALUES (10, 2024, 10, 63000); INSERT INTO monthly_revenue VALUES (11, 2024, 11, 68000); INSERT INTO monthly_revenue VALUES (12, 2024, 12, 72000);
broken_query: SELECT year, month, amount, mom_change FROM (SELECT year, month, amount, amount - LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY amount) AS mom_change FROM monthly_revenue) WHERE mom_change IS NOT NULL ORDER BY year, month
expected_query: SELECT year, month, amount, mom_change FROM (SELECT year, month, amount, amount - LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY year, month) AS mom_change FROM monthly_revenue) WHERE mom_change IS NOT NULL ORDER BY year, month
schema_description:
Table: monthly_revenue
  - id (INTEGER): Primary key
  - year (INTEGER): Calendar year
  - month (INTEGER): Calendar month (1-12)
  - amount (REAL): Total revenue for that month
expected_description: Returns 11 rows (months 2-12) with the month-over-month revenue change. Feb should show -4000 (decline from Jan), Mar +7000, Apr +6000, May -3000, Jun +7000, Jul -3000, Aug +8000, Sep -3000, Oct +6000, Nov +5000, Dec +4000.

description: Find employees in the top 3 salary tiers per department. The broken query uses RANK() which skips rank numbers after ties, causing employees at the 3rd distinct salary level to receive rank 4 (not 3) and be incorrectly excluded. DENSE_RANK() must be used instead.
schema_sql: CREATE TABLE employees (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL, salary REAL NOT NULL);
seed_data_sql: INSERT INTO employees VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'Engineering', 100000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (2, 'Bob', 'Engineering', 90000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (3, 'Charlie', 'Engineering', 90000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (4, 'Dave', 'Engineering', 80000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (5, 'Eve', 'Engineering', 70000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (6, 'Frank', 'Marketing', 75000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (7, 'Grace', 'Marketing', 65000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (8, 'Henry', 'Marketing', 65000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (9, 'Iris', 'Marketing', 55000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (10, 'Jack', 'Marketing', 45000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (11, 'Kate', 'Finance', 88000); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (12, 'Leo', 'Finance', 78000);
broken_query: SELECT name, department, salary FROM (SELECT name, department, salary, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rnk FROM employees) ranked WHERE rnk <= 3 ORDER BY department, salary DESC
expected_query: SELECT name, department, salary FROM (SELECT name, department, salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dr FROM employees) ranked WHERE dr <= 3 ORDER BY department, salary DESC
schema_description:
Table: employees
  - id (INTEGER): Primary key
  - name (TEXT): Employee name
  - department (TEXT): Department (Engineering, Marketing, Finance)
  - salary (REAL): Annual salary
expected_description: Returns employees in the top 3 distinct salary tiers per department using DENSE_RANK. Should return 10 rows: Engineering has 4 employees (Alice 100000, Bob/Charlie 90000, Dave 80000), Marketing has 4 (Frank 75000, Grace/Henry 65000, Iris 55000), Finance has 2 (Kate 88000, Leo 78000).

description: Using a CTE with a self-join, find employees who earn more than their direct manager. The broken query reverses the comparison (salary < mgr_salary), returning subordinates who earn LESS than their manager — the opposite of what is required.
schema_sql: CREATE TABLE employees (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL, salary REAL NOT NULL, manager_id INTEGER);
seed_data_sql: INSERT INTO employees VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'Executive', 150000, NULL); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (2, 'Bob', 'Engineering', 95000, 1); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (3, 'Carol', 'Engineering', 102000, 2); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (4, 'Dave', 'Engineering', 85000, 2); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (5, 'Eve', 'Marketing', 80000, 1); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (6, 'Frank', 'Marketing', 65000, 5); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (7, 'Grace', 'Marketing', 85000, 5); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (8, 'Henry', 'Finance', 90000, 1); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (9, 'Iris', 'Finance', 95000, 8); INSERT INTO employees VALUES (10, 'Jack', 'Finance', 88000, 8);
broken_query: WITH mgr_salaries AS (SELECT e.id, e.name, e.salary, m.salary AS mgr_salary FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id) SELECT name FROM mgr_salaries WHERE salary < mgr_salary ORDER BY name
expected_query: WITH mgr_salaries AS (SELECT e.id, e.name, e.salary, m.salary AS mgr_salary FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id) SELECT name FROM mgr_salaries WHERE salary > mgr_salary ORDER BY name
schema_description:
Table: employees
  - id (INTEGER): Primary key
  - name (TEXT): Employee name
  - department (TEXT): Department name
  - salary (REAL): Annual salary
  - manager_id (INTEGER): Foreign key referencing the employee's direct manager (NULL for top-level)
expected_description: Returns names of employees who earn strictly more than their direct manager. Should return 3 rows (alphabetical): Carol (102000 > Bob's 95000), Grace (85000 > Eve's 80000), Iris (95000 > Henry's 90000).
